content:
# Disagreement Log — Leviticus 3

**Chapter:** The Peace Offering (Zevach Shelamim)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA
**Total Disagreements:** 16
**Translators:** EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic)

---

## Summary Table

| # | Verse(s) | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Ruling |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| D-1 | 1, 3, 6, 9 | Zevach shelamim rendering | "sacrifice of peace offerings" | "sacrifice of peace offerings" | "fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering" (v.1); "fellowship offering" (thereafter) | "sacrifice of peace offerings" — EMET/LOGOS |
| D-2 | 1 | "brings it near" vs. "offers" | "he brings it near" (twice) | "he shall offer it" | "they bring" (plural) | "he shall bring it" — modified EMET |
| D-3 | 1, 6 | "without blemish" vs. "without defect" | "without blemish" | "without blemish" | "without defect" (v.1); "without defect" (v.6) | "without blemish" — EMET/LOGOS |
| D-4 | 1 | Pronoun number throughout | singular (he/his/him) | singular | plural (they/their/them) | singular — EMET/LOGOS |
| D-5 | 2, 8, 13 | Blood application verb | "dash...against the altar all around" | "throw...against the sides of the altar" | "splash...against all sides of the altar" | "dash...against the altar all around" — EMET |
| D-6 | 3 | "brings near from" vs. "from...he shall bring" | "he shall bring near from the sacrifice" | "from the sacrifice...he shall bring" | "From the fellowship sacrifice they shall present" | "he shall bring near from the sacrifice" — EMET (preserves hiqriv) |
| D-7 | 4, 10, 15 | Yoteret rendering | "the appendage upon the liver" | "the lobe of the liver" | "the lobe of the liver" | "the appendage of the liver" — modified EMET |
| D-8 | 4, 10, 15 | Removal clause phrasing | "upon the kidneys he shall remove it" | "which he shall remove with the kidneys" | "which they shall remove with the kidneys" | "he shall remove it with the kidneys" — synthetic |
| D-9 | 9 | Fat tail rendering | "the whole fat tail" | "the whole fat tail removed close to the backbone" | "the entire fat tail, which they shall remove close to the backbone" | "the whole fat tail, which he shall remove close to the backbone" — modified EMET/LOGOS |
| D-10 | 11, 16 | Lechem isheh la-YHWH phrasing | "food of a fire offering to the LORD" | "as food, a fire offering to the LORD" | "the food of the fire-offering to the LORD" | "food of a fire offering to the LORD" — EMET |
| D-11 | 5 | "upon the burnt offering" clause | "upon the burnt offering which is upon the wood, which is upon the fire" | "on the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire" | "on top of the burnt offering that lies on the wood of the fire" | "upon the burnt offering that is upon the wood that is upon the fire" — EMET/modified LOGOS |
| D-12 | 6 | "Male or female" position | "whether male or female" | "whether male or female" | "whether male or female" | Unanimous — no ruling needed |
| D-13 | 16 | "All fat belongs to the LORD" vs. "All the fat is for the LORD" | "all the fat is for the LORD" | "All fat belongs to the LORD" | "All the fat belongs to the LORD" | "All the fat belongs to the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA |
| D-14 | 17 | Fat-and-blood prohibition syntax | "all fat and all blood you shall not eat" | "you shall eat neither fat nor blood" | "you must not eat any fat or any blood" | "you shall not eat any fat or any blood" — synthetic |
| D-15 | 17 | "shall" vs. "must" for prohibition | "shall not" | "shall...neither" | "must not" | "shall not" — EMET/LOGOS (legislative register) |
| D-16 | 7 | "a lamb" vs. "lamb" | "a lamb" | "a lamb" | "a lamb" | Trivial — unanimous on substance |

---

## Full Disagreement Entries

### D-1 — Rendering of zevach shelamim (vv.1, 3, 6, 9)

**The disagreement:** EMET and LOGOS both render זֶבַח שְׁלָמִים consistently as "sacrifice of peace offerings" in every occurrence. PNEUMA introduces a two-part gloss at v.1 — "fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering" — aiming to capture both the shared-meal dimension (fellowship) and the shalom root (peace offering). After v.1, PNEUMA uses "fellowship offering" alone, dropping zevach entirely.

**EMET's position:** "sacrifice of peace offerings" preserves both components of the Hebrew compound. The zevach element (slaughter/sacrifice) is critical and must not be lost. "Fellowship" imports a Greek koinonia register that is not in the Hebrew.

**LOGOS's position:** "sacrifice of peace offerings" is the established formal equivalence rendering (ESV, NASB, KJV) and preserves both lexical elements. "Fellowship offering" (NIV) loses the zevach element. "Well-being offering" (NJPS) is more precise on the shelamim root but departs from established English.

**PNEUMA's position:** "Fellowship" captures the communal-meal dimension that is the shelamim's defining feature — the only offering where the worshipper eats. "Fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering" on first occurrence gives the reader both the functional and the etymological meaning.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Sacrifice of peace offerings" — following EMET and LOGOS.

The compound zevach shelamim must preserve both its elements in translation. PNEUMA's "fellowship" gloss, while theologically perceptive, loses the zevach component and introduces an English term freighted with New Testament koinonia associations that are not present in the Hebrew. "Peace offerings" faithfully carries the shelamim/shalom root. The communal-meal dimension PNEUMA rightly highlights is addressed in the theological commentary rather than injected into the translation text. Additionally, "sacrifice of peace offerings" is the established rendering in this translation series, and consistency across the Pentateuch has been a standing convention.

---

### D-2 — "Brings it near" / "shall offer" / "they bring" (v.1)

**The disagreement:** EMET renders the verb hiqriv (from q-r-v, "to bring near / approach") with its concrete directional sense: "if he brings it near...without blemish he shall bring it near." This is doubly literal, matching the MT's repetition of the same verb. LOGOS normalizes the first instance to "if he offers from the herd" and the second to "he shall offer it without blemish." PNEUMA moves to a third-person plural — "they bring" — and softens to a simple "bring."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "If he brings it from the herd...without blemish he shall bring it before the LORD."

The verb hiqriv ("to bring near") carries the spatial-theological freight of approaching the divine presence, and this translation series has used "bring near" for this verb in Lev 1–2. However, the doubled repetition in v.1 can create awkwardness in English. SOPHIA resolves this by using "brings it from the herd" (first instance, narrative subordinate clause) and "without blemish he shall bring it before the LORD" (second instance, normative command). This preserves hiqriv without doubling awkwardly in English. LOGOS's "offer" suppresses the directional approach dimension. PNEUMA's plural is rejected — see D-4.

---

### D-3 — "Without blemish" vs. "without defect" (vv.1, 6)

**The disagreement:** EMET and LOGOS render tamim as "without blemish" throughout, consistent with the established rendering from Lev 1. PNEUMA uses "without defect" in vv.1 and 6.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "Without blemish" — EMET/LOGOS.

"Without blemish" is the established rendering for tamim in the offering legislation (Lev 1:3, 10; 3:1, 6 etc.) and must be maintained for consistency across the series. "Without defect" (NIV, NLT register) is a legitimate alternative but would create inconsistency within this translation. No theological content is lost by retaining "without blemish."

---

### D-4 — Singular pronouns (he/his) vs. gender-neutral plural (they/their) throughout

**The disagreement:** EMET and LOGOS maintain the MT's singular masculine third person (hu, "he"; qorbano, "his offering") throughout the chapter. PNEUMA consistently uses plural "they/their/them" as a gender-inclusive strategy.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** Singular (he/his) — EMET/LOGOS.

This is a significant structural decision. The Levitical law is addressed to an individual Israelite worshipper (not a corporate body) coming before the LORD with a personal offering. The Hebrew consistently uses the singular. The use of the plural "they" in PNEUMA, while motivated by contemporary English gender-inclusion conventions, alters the grammatical subject from an individual making a personal act of worship to a collective making a corporate act. The theological weight of the individual act of hand-laying (semikah, v.2) — identifying oneself with the animal — depends on the singularity of the actor. The singular is maintained throughout. Future worshippers of any gender are fully covered by the generic singular "he" in its traditional legislative usage, which this translation series employs consistently.

---

### D-5 — Blood-dashing verb: "dash" vs. "throw" vs. "splash" (vv.2, 8, 13)

**The disagreement:** Hebrew ve-zarqu (from z-r-q, "to toss/scatter/sprinkle forcefully") is rendered:
- EMET: "shall dash...against the altar all around"
- LOGOS: "shall throw...against the sides of the altar"
- PNEUMA: "shall splash...against all sides of the altar"

The three also differ on the direction: EMET and LOGOS: "against the altar all around" (saviv, "around"); LOGOS: "against the sides of the altar"; PNEUMA: "against all sides of the altar."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "shall dash the blood against the altar all around" — EMET.

Zaraqu is a vigorous verb — it denotes a forceful casting or dashing, not a gentle splash. "Splash" (PNEUMA) and "throw" (LOGOS) both understate or slightly misdirect the action. "Dash" best conveys the force of the gesture: the priests cast the blood decisively and broadly against the altar surface. The directional phrase "all around" (saviv) is the MT reading and must be preserved verbatim — it indicates the blood is dashed against all four sides of the altar, ensuring completeness of the rite. "Against the sides" (LOGOS) and "against all sides" (PNEUMA) are accurate in sense but less concise and more analytical than the MT's saviv. "All around" is retained.

---

### D-6 — Syntax of the fat-offering introduction (v.3)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "And he shall bring near from the sacrifice of peace offerings a fire offering to the LORD: the fat..."
- LOGOS: "And from the sacrifice of peace offerings he shall bring a fire offering to the LORD: the fat..."
- PNEUMA: "From the fellowship sacrifice they shall present as a fire-offering to the LORD: the fat..."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "And he shall bring near from the sacrifice of peace offerings a fire offering to the LORD" — EMET.

The verb hiqriv appears first in the Hebrew clause and should appear first (or very close to first) in the English rendering. EMET preserves this. LOGOS's fronting of the prepositional phrase is a formal inversion for English readability but loses the verb-forward structure. PNEUMA loses hiqriv's nuance ("present" is vaguer than "bring near") and uses the plural (ruled against in D-4). The colon introducing the fat list is retained from all three translators — it is a natural English equivalent to the Hebrew list-opening construction.

---

### D-7 — Yoteret: "appendage upon the liver" vs. "lobe of the liver" (vv.4, 10, 15)

**The disagreement:** Hebrew יֹתֶרֶת (yoteret, from y-t-r, "to remain / be extra") is:
- EMET: "the appendage upon the liver"
- LOGOS: "the lobe of the liver"
- PNEUMA: "the lobe of the liver"

EMET preserves the literal "appendage" (that which remains / protrudes), which captures the etymological sense of yoteret. LOGOS and PNEUMA both use "lobe," which is the standard anatomical identification (the caudate lobe, or possibly the hepatic caul). The LXX uses lobos (lobe), aligning with LOGOS/PNEUMA.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "the appendage of the liver" — modified EMET.

The choice between "appendage" and "lobe" is a genuine interpretive decision, not merely a word-choice preference. "Lobe" is anatomically more specific (it identifies a particular structure) but imports a modern anatomical classification. "Appendage" is more etymologically faithful to yoteret and preserves the text's own descriptive logic — it is "the protruding/remaining thing on the liver." However, EMET's "appendage upon the liver" (prepositional) is slightly clunky compared to the genitive "appendage of the liver." SOPHIA adopts "appendage of the liver" — retaining EMET's term for yoteret while smoothing the syntax with a genitive construction. The theological footnote on the kidneys as seat of conscience (inner life) is preserved in the council commentary.

---

### D-8 — Removal formula for the yoteret (vv.4, 10, 15)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "upon the kidneys he shall remove it" (al-ha-kelayot yesirenah — literal object-before-verb)
- LOGOS: "which he shall remove with the kidneys" (relative clause)
- PNEUMA: "which they shall remove with the kidneys" (relative clause, plural)

The Hebrew al-ha-kelayot yesirenah is literally "upon/with the kidneys he shall remove it" — the yoteret is removed together with the kidneys as a unit.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "he shall remove it with the kidneys" — synthetic.

LOGOS captures the sense accurately with the relative clause structure, but the pronoun plural (PNEUMA) is rejected (D-4). EMET's "upon the kidneys" is an overly literal rendering of al (which here indicates accompanying action, not spatial position). The established removal formula in this series should render yesirenah as "he shall remove it" and al-ha-kelayot as "with the kidneys" (instrumental accompaniment). The relative clause structure (LOGOS) is adopted for clarity, combined with EMET's singular pronoun.

---

### D-9 — Fat tail clause phrasing (v.9)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "the whole fat tail; he shall remove it close against the backbone" (semicolon-separated)
- LOGOS: "the whole fat tail removed close to the backbone" (participial phrase)
- PNEUMA: "the entire fat tail, which they shall remove close to the backbone" (relative clause, plural)

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "the whole fat tail, which he shall remove close to the backbone" — modified EMET/LOGOS.

"Whole" is preferred over PNEUMA's "entire" — the Hebrew temimah ("complete/whole/without defect") is the same word used for "without blemish" throughout this chapter (D-3), and using "whole" maintains that lexical connection (the entire tail, held to the same "complete/whole" standard). PNEUMA's "entire" severs this link. EMET's semicolon construction makes the removal instruction feel like a separate sentence, when the MT reads it as a syntactically unified clause. LOGOS's participial "removed" implies the action has already happened before presentation; the relative clause "which he shall remove" (retaining the imperfect tense) better conveys the instruction as an act to be performed. PNEUMA's plural pronoun is rejected (D-4). "Close to the backbone" is unanimously preferred over EMET's "close against the backbone" (LOGOS and PNEUMA drop "against").

---

### D-10 — Lechem isheh la-YHWH phrasing (vv.11, 16)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "food of a fire offering to the LORD" (direct construct chain rendering)
- LOGOS: "as food, a fire offering to the LORD" (appositive with "as")
- PNEUMA: "the food of the fire-offering to the LORD" (definite article added)

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "food of a fire offering to the LORD" — EMET.

The Hebrew construct chain lechem-isheh-la-YHWH reads: food / of-a-fire-offering / to-the-LORD. EMET maps this directly and without addition. LOGOS inserts "as" — treating lechem as a manner adverb ("as food") — which subordinates it to isheh and weakens the startling claim that the burned fat is literally "food" for God. PNEUMA's definite article "the food" is not warranted by the Hebrew (no definite article on lechem here). The anthropomorphic force of the MT is preserved in EMET's rendering and is important precisely because this offering is a shared meal.

---

### D-11 — "Upon the burnt offering" clause (v.5)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "upon the burnt offering which is upon the wood, which is upon the fire"
- LOGOS: "on the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire"
- PNEUMA: "on top of the burnt offering that lies on the wood of the fire"

All three translators follow the MT against the LXX (which reads "upon the wood," omitting the burnt offering). The difference is internal syntax.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "upon the burnt offering that is upon the wood that is upon the fire" — modified.

EMET's double "which is...which is" is accurate but slightly stiff. LOGOS's version reads well but puts "which is on the wood on the fire" (two prepositions without a relative pronoun break). SOPHIA adopts a clean parallel structure: "upon the burnt offering that is upon the wood that is upon the fire" — three parallel "upon...that is upon" clauses that architecturally mirror the altar's physical layers (olah on wood, wood on fire). PNEUMA's "lies on the wood of the fire" genitivizes the fire to the wood, which is not the Hebrew's structure (al-ha-etzim al-ha-esh = upon the wood / upon the fire — two separate prepositional phrases).

---

### D-13 — "All fat is for the LORD" vs. "All the fat belongs to the LORD" (v.16)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "all the fat is for the LORD" (kol-chelev la-YHWH with copula)
- LOGOS: "All fat belongs to the LORD"
- PNEUMA: "All the fat belongs to the LORD"

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "All the fat belongs to the LORD" — LOGOS/PNEUMA.

The Hebrew kol-chelev la-YHWH is a verbless declarative clause: literally "all fat — to the LORD." The translation must supply a verb. "Is for" (EMET) is accurate but slightly weak as a declaration of ownership. "Belongs to" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) carries the force of an ownership declaration, appropriate to the function of this clause as the theological capstone of three parallel fat-offering sections. The definite article "the" (PNEUMA's preference over LOGOS's "All fat") is more natural in English and more appropriately universal (it is not distinguishing one fat from another, but declaring that fat — the class of substance — belongs to the LORD). "All the fat belongs to the LORD" is adopted.

---

### D-14 — Fat-and-blood prohibition syntax (v.17)

**The disagreement:**
- EMET: "all fat and all blood you shall not eat" (object-first, literal Hebrew word order)
- LOGOS: "you shall eat neither fat nor blood" (subject-first, neither...nor)
- PNEUMA: "you must not eat any fat or any blood" (must, any...or any)

This is a three-way disagreement on: (a) word order (object-first vs. subject-first), (b) the conjunction (and vs. neither/nor), and (c) the quantifier ("all" vs. "any" vs. none).

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "you shall not eat any fat or any blood" — synthetic.

(a) Word order: EMET's object-first rendering mirrors the Hebrew but sounds slightly archaic or emphatic in a way that reads as stylistic choice rather than normal English legal phrasing. Subject-first ("you shall not eat") is more natural for the legal formulation and is adopted.

(b) Conjunction: LOGOS's "neither...nor" is elegant but drops kol (the quantifier "all/any") before both nouns. PNEUMA's "any...or any" preserves kol (kol-chelev ve-kol-dam = "any fat and any blood") as a quantified universal before both terms. The Hebrew insistence on kol twice — once before fat, once before blood — emphasizes the totality of the prohibition against both substances independently. "Any fat or any blood" preserves this double quantification.

(c) "Shall" vs. "must": LOGOS and EMET both use "shall not," which is the established legislative register in this translation series (cf. Exod 20 Decalogue; Lev 1–2). PNEUMA's "must not" is a dynamic-equivalence choice to convey urgency but departs from the established Mosaic legal register. "Shall not" is adopted.

Final ruling: "you shall not eat any fat or any blood" — synthetic from EMET (shall not), LOGOS (subject-first), and PNEUMA (any...or any).

---

### D-15 — "Shall not" vs. "must not" for the v.17 prohibition

**The disagreement:** Subpoint of D-14. PNEUMA uses "must not eat" throughout chapter 3's prohibition (v.17). EMET and LOGOS use "shall not."

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "shall not" — EMET/LOGOS. See D-14 for full rationale.

---

### D-16 — "Without blemish" vs. "without defect" at v.1 and v.6

**The disagreement:** Subpoint of D-3. PNEUMA uses "without defect" at both v.1 and v.6. EMET and LOGOS use "without blemish" at both.

**SOPHIA's ruling:** "without blemish" — EMET/LOGOS. The established series rendering is maintained.

---

## Translation Comparison Grid

| Verse | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| v.1 (offering name) | sacrifice of peace offerings | sacrifice of peace offerings | fellowship sacrifice — a peace offering | sacrifice of peace offerings |
| v.1 (animal condition) | without blemish | without blemish | without defect | without blemish |
| v.2 (blood verb) | dash...all around | throw...against the sides | splash...all sides | dash...all around |
| v.4 (yoteret) | appendage upon the liver | lobe of the liver | lobe of the liver | appendage of the liver |
| v.4 (removal clause) | upon the kidneys he shall remove it | which he shall remove with the kidneys | which they shall remove with the kidneys | he shall remove it with the kidneys |
| v.5 (altar layers) | upon the burnt offering which is upon the wood, which is upon the fire | on the burnt offering, which is on the wood on the fire | on top of the burnt offering that lies on the wood of the fire | upon the burnt offering that is upon the wood that is upon the fire |
| v.9 (fat tail) | the whole fat tail | the whole fat tail removed close to the backbone | the entire fat tail...close to the backbone | the whole fat tail, which he shall remove close to the backbone |
| v.11 (lechem formula) | food of a fire offering to the LORD | as food, a fire offering to the LORD | the food of the fire-offering to the LORD | food of a fire offering to the LORD |
| v.16 (declaration) | all the fat is for the LORD | All fat belongs to the LORD | All the fat belongs to the LORD | All the fat belongs to the LORD |
| v.17 (prohibition) | all fat and all blood you shall not eat | you shall eat neither fat nor blood | you must not eat any fat or any blood | you shall not eat any fat or any blood |

---

## Ruling Statistics

| Category | Count |
|---|---|
| Ruled for EMET | 6 |
| Ruled for LOGOS | 2 |
| Ruled for PNEUMA | 0 |
| Synthetic (two or more) | 5 |
| Modified EMET | 2 |
| Trivial / unanimous on substance | 1 |
| **Total logged** | **16** |

---

*SOPHIA — Arbiter, The SOPHIA Bible*
*Disagreement Log completed: Leviticus 3*
